disease.name: Congenital TORCH Infections Presenting with Jaundice
disease.note: TORCH: Toxoplasmosis, Other (syphilis, VZV, Parvovirus B19, HIV, Zika, Enterovirus), Rubella, CMV, HSV. Congenital infections that cause neonatal jaundice through hepatitis, hemolysis, or cholestasis. CMV is the most common congenital infection (0.5-2% of live births) and the most common TORCH cause of neonatal jaundice.

immediateDangerSigns.sign: seizures in first 48 hours of life (HSV encephalitis, toxoplasmosis)
vesicular rash (HSV, VZV) requiring immediate acyclovir
septic appearance with respiratory distress and fulminant hepatitis (HSV)

severityAssessment.severityLabel: All congenital TORCH infections require urgent evaluation and specific treatment. Severity ranges from asymptomatic (common in CMV) to life-threatening (HSV, severe CMV, toxoplasmosis).

historyFeatures.symptomId: jaundice in first 24-48 hours of life
maternal infection during pregnancy (may be asymptomatic)
hearing concerns or failed newborn hearing screen

firstLine: Neonatal IgM-specific antibodies for each TORCH agent (cord blood)
Blood PCR for CMV, HSV, toxoplasmosis, parvovirus B19, Zika
Urine CMV PCR (sensitive for congenital CMV)
Cerebrospinal fluid analysis and PCR (CMV, HSV, enterovirus)
Liver function tests and fractionated bilirubin
Complete blood count with differential and platelet count
Cranial ultrasound (periventricular calcifications, ventriculomegaly, hemorrhage, cystic changes)
secondLine: Head CT without contrast (if cranial US abnormal or equivocal for calcifications)
Long bone X-rays (metaphyseal lucencies in rubella and syphilis, periostitis in syphilis)
Echocardiogram (ruling out PDA, pulmonary artery stenosis in rubella)
Placental pathology with histology and PCR (characteristic viral inclusions)
Neuroimaging MRI for detailed brain assessment
investigations.goldStandard: Positive neonatal blood or tissue PCR plus elevated specific IgM from cord blood with consistent clinical syndrome and maternal serologic evidence of primary infection during pregnancy

emergency: IV acyclovir (60 mg/kg/day divided q8h) for suspected HSV - do not wait for confirmation
IV penicillin G (50,000 units/kg/dose q12h x 10-14 days) for congenital syphilis
IVIG 1 g/kg for severe parvovirus B19 anemia or hydrops
VZIG (varicella zoster immune globulin) plus IV acyclovir for congenital VZV
Supportive care for seizures, respiratory distress, shock as needed
inpatient: Congenital CMV moderate-severe: Valganciclovir (16 mg/kg/dose q12h) or IV ganciclovir (6 mg/kg/dose q12h) x 6 weeks to 6 months
Congenital toxoplasmosis: Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin for 12 months (monitor for myelosuppression)
Congenital HSV: IV acyclovir x 14-21 days (skin/eye/mouth: 14 days, CNS/disseminated: 21 days) then suppressive oral acyclovir x 6 months
Congenital rubella: Supportive care, isolation precautions (contagious in first year)
Congenital syphilis: IV penicillin G x 10-14 days, monitor for Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction
Congenital parvovirus B19: IVIG for severe anemia, red cell transfusion if needed
Congenital Zika: Supportive care, seizure management, long-term monitoring
outpatient: Long-term neurodevelopmental follow-up for all affected infants
Serial audiologic assessments (CMV, toxoplasmosis, rubella - progressive hearing loss possible)
Regular ophthalmologic follow-up (chorioretinitis, cataract, glaucoma surveillance)
Valganciclovir continuation for CMV (total 6 months) with CBC monitoring for neutropenia
Pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine continuation for toxoplasmosis with hematologic monitoring
Anticipatory guidance for specific syndrome manifestations
Immunizations per schedule (reassess if on immunosuppressive therapy)
refractory: Consider resistance testing if not responding to antiviral therapy (ganciclovir-resistant CMV: foscarnet or cidofovir)
Liver transplant for progressive liver failure in severe congenital CMV or toxoplasmosis with cirrhosis
preventive: Maternal TORCH screening in early pregnancy (serologies)
Avoidance of cat litter and undercooked meat during pregnancy (toxoplasmosis prevention)
Safe sexual practices and syphilis screening during pregnancy
CMV prevention: hand hygiene, avoiding sharing utensils with young children
HSV: suppressive acyclovir in third trimester for mothers with active HSV lesions
Zika: mosquito precautions and avoidance of travel to endemic areas during pregnancy

disease.pathophysiology: Transplacental transmission of pathogen during pregnancy leads to hematogenous dissemination in the fetus with multiorgan involvement. The pathogens demonstrate particular tropism for: liver (hepatitis, cholestasis, giant cell transformation), brain (microcephaly, periventricular calcifications with CMV, diffuse calcifications and hydrocephalus with toxoplasmosis, intracerebral hemorrhages with HSV), eye (chorioretinitis most common in CMV and toxoplasmosis, cataract in rubella, micropthalmia in CMV), bone marrow (thrombocytopenia causing petechiae/purpura, hemolytic anemia with parvovirus B19), and inner ear (sensorineural hearing loss especially CMV, rubella). The resulting chronic intrauterine infection causes damage that is highly dependent on gestational age at time of infection, with first trimester infections producing the most severe manifestations.
complications: Severe neurodevelopmental delay and cerebral palsy
Sensorineural hearing loss (most common sequela of congenital CMV)
Visual impairment from chorioretinitis, cataract, or optic atrophy
Growth restriction and failure to thrive
mustNotMiss: Neonatal HSV - rapidly fatal without treatment, start IV acyclovir empirically if suspected even before PCR confirmation
Congenital CMV - most common infectious cause of sensorineural hearing loss, treatable with valganciclovir
Congenital syphilis - fully treatable if detected, preventable with maternal screening
Do NOT attribute early jaundice (first 24-48h) to physiological causes until TORCH and hemolytic workup is negative
Maternal history may be completely negative - many primary TORCH infections are asymptomatic

diagnosticClues.clue: SGA/IUGR with hepatosplenomegaly, petechiae/purpura, and jaundice in first 24h of life
Chorioretinitis plus hydrocephalus plus diffuse intracranial calcifications = toxoplasmosis
Periventricular calcifications plus sensorineural hearing loss plus microcephaly = CMV
Cataract plus patent ductus arteriosus plus radiolucent metaphyseal bone lesions = rubella
Vesicular rash on erythematous base in clusters plus seizures = HSV
Snuffles (profuse purulent nasal discharge) with periostitis and metaphyseal lucencies = syphilis
Hydrops fetalis with severe anemia = parvovirus B19

exclusionClues.clue: Negative maternal TORCH serologies for IgM and IgG in pregnancy
Negative neonatal blood and urine PCR for CMV, HSV, toxoplasmosis, parvovirus B19, Zika
Normal head imaging (no calcifications, ventriculomegaly, or hemorrhages)
Normal ophthalmologic exam (no chorioretinitis, cataract, or micropthalmia)
Physiological jaundice pattern (unconjugated, peaks day 3-5, healthy active infant without hepatosplenomegaly)
Negative CSF PCR for HSV and enterovirus in infant with suspected CNS infection
